claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to start by asking about the child or children you're claiming for.

**First, let me ask: How old is the child (or children) you're asking about?**

The age categories are:
- **Under 16** - generally automatically qualifies
- **16 to 19** - may qualify if in approved education or training
- **20 or over** - generally not eligible

Please tell me the age(s) of your child/children, and if you have more than one child, let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm asking about my daughter Alex - she's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're asking about your daughter Alex, who is 8 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, she falls into the category that generally qualifies for Child Benefit. Let me continue with the next question.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Given that Alex is only 8 years old, I would expect she's not in paid work at all. Can you confirm this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, she doesn't work at all - she's just a regular 8-year-old at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming that. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in her own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?**

In other words, can you confirm that Alex is not claiming benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in her own name? (Again, for an 8-year-old this would be very unusual, so I expect the answer is that she's not receiving these benefits.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she doesn't receive any benefits in her own name - nothing like that at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?**

Given that Alex is 8 years old and at school, I assume she's not in any apprenticeship program. Can you confirm this?

(For context, certain apprenticeships can affect Child Benefit eligibility for older teenagers, but this wouldn't apply to an 8-year-old in regular school.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - she's not in any apprenticeship. She's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me move on to questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to ask about your residency status:**

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit?**

You qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself financially (and not claiming certain benefits)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** The most common situation is simply that you normally live in the UK. Please let me know your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Living in the UK meets the residency requirement. Now let me ask about your relationship with Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are you responsible for Alex?**

You're considered responsible if ANY of these apply:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the amount of Child Benefit towards her care - for things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit amount

**Which of these applies to your situation?** Do you live with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, she lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That clearly establishes responsibility. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question: Is anyone else already claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else who might be claiming

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. 

**What's your situation?**
- Is no one else claiming for Alex?
- Is someone else claiming but you've agreed they will transfer it to you?
- Is there a dispute about who should claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for her.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Where is Alex currently living?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply:

- Is Alex **in hospital**?
- Is Alex **in local authority care**?
- Is Alex **fostered**?
- Or is Alex **living normally at home with you**?

Given that you mentioned she lives at home with you, I assume she's living normally at home. Can you confirm this, and that she's not currently in hospital or in any care arrangement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually she has been in hospital for a while now - she's been in the hospital ward for about three and a half months. But I still buy her clothes, magazines, and her favorite snacks and things.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for sharing that - I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital. It's clear you're still very much involved in her care. Let me check the rules about hospital stays.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. This is an important detail. Let me ask some specific questions about Alex's hospital stay:

**Has Alex been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (3 months) cumulatively?**

You mentioned she's been there for about three and a half months. Let me clarify the rules:

- Child Benefit continues for up to **12 weeks** while a child is in hospital
- If the stay exceeds 12 weeks, you can still claim IF you're regularly spending money on the child (which you are - buying clothes, magazines, snacks)

So to be clear:
1. Has Alex been in hospital continuously for about 3.5 months (which would be approximately **14 weeks**)?
2. Has she had any breaks from hospital during this time (came home for more than 28 days at any point)?

This will help me determine which rules apply to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, she's been there continuously for about three and a half months - so that would be around 14 weeks, I think. She hasn't come home during that time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. So Alex has been in hospital continuously for about 14 weeks, which is over the 12-week threshold. However, the good news is that you're still regularly spending money on her care (clothes, magazines, snacks), which is an important factor. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! Now I need to confirm: Are you regularly spending money on Alex while she is in hospital?**

You mentioned you're buying her clothes, magazines, and her favorite snacks. This counts as regularly spending money on her care, which is the important exception to the 12-week hospital rule.

Can you confirm that yes, you are regularly spending money on Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - I'm still buying things for her regularly while she's in hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's an important exception that allows you to continue claiming. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, can you confirm that the local council is not paying for Alex's care or accommodation costs?

(Hospital care is typically funded by the NHS rather than the local council, but I need to confirm this to complete the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the local council isn't paying anything towards her care. It's just the NHS hospital treatment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That completes the assessment. Let me get the final outcome information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit

**Result: ELIGIBLE** ✓

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for your daughter Alex.

### Why You're Eligible:

Even though Alex has been in hospital for about 14 weeks (which is over the standard 12-week limit), **you still qualify because you're regularly spending money on her** while she's in hospital. The clothes, magazines, and snacks you're buying for her are exactly the type of regular spending that allows you to continue claiming Child Benefit beyond the 12-week hospital threshold.

Additionally, you meet all the other requirements:
- Alex is 8 years old (under 16)
- She's not working or receiving benefits in her own right
- You live in the UK (Manchester)
- You're responsible for Alex (she normally lives with you)
- No one else is claiming for her
- The local council isn't paying for her care

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** if you or your partner earn over £60,000
5. **Payments are made every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can apply here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

I hope Alex recovers well and is home soon. If you have any other questions, please feel free to ask!

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) She is 8 years old, which is under 16 and automatically qualifies age-wise. (2) She is not working 24+ hours per week. (3) She is not receiving benefits in her own right. (4) She is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester). (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 14 weeks (over the 12-week threshold), the claimant continues to be eligible because they are regularly spending money on Alex while she is in hospital (buying clothes, magazines, and snacks), which is an important exception to the 12-week hospital rule. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their daughter Alex. Despite Alex's extended hospital stay of 14 weeks, the claimant qualifies under the exception that allows Child Benefit to continue beyond 12 weeks when the parent is regularly spending money on the child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

(3) She is not receiving benefits in her own right. (4) She is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester). (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 14 weeks (over the 12-week threshold), the claimant continues to be eligible because they are regularly spending money on Alex while she is in hospital (buying clothes, magazines, and snacks), which is an important exception to the 12-week hospital rule. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their daughter Alex. Despite Alex's extended hospital stay of 14 weeks, the claimant qualifies under the exception that allows Child Benefit to continue beyond 12 weeks when the parent is regularly spending money on the child.
